historical_instances.context: Arab oil embargo following the Yom Kippur War. Oil prices quadrupled from $3 to $12 per barrel.
historical_instances.equity_behavior: S&P 500 crashed over 40% peak-to-trough as inflation surged and the economy entered a severe recession.
historical_instances.rates_behavior: The Federal Reserve hiked interest rates aggressively to combat double-digit inflation, exacerbating the economic contraction.
historical_instances.resolution: The embargo was lifted in March 1974 after diplomatic negotiations, but the economic damage and stagflation persisted for years.

historical_instances.context: First Gulf War following Iraq's invasion of Kuwait. Oil prices spiked from $17 to $40 per barrel.
historical_instances.equity_behavior: S&P 500 fell nearly 20% in the immediate aftermath of the invasion as recession fears mounted.
historical_instances.rates_behavior: The Fed had already been tightening, but the oil shock tipped the US into a mild recession.
historical_instances.resolution: Oil prices collapsed back to pre-war levels once the US-led coalition launched Operation Desert Storm in early 1991, sparking a massive equity rally.

historical_instances.context: Commodity supercycle peak. Oil surged to an all-time high of $147 per barrel in July 2008.
historical_instances.equity_behavior: Equities were already weakening due to the subprime mortgage crisis, but the oil spike acted as a massive tax on consumers, accelerating the crash.
historical_instances.rates_behavior: The Fed was cutting rates to fight the housing bust, but the oil spike temporarily complicated their inflation mandate.
historical_instances.resolution: Demand destruction from the Great Financial Crisis caused oil to crash from $147 to $32 by late 2008.

historical_instances.context: Russia's invasion of Ukraine triggered a massive supply shock. Oil spiked above $130 per barrel.
historical_instances.equity_behavior: S&P 500 entered a bear market, falling over 20% as the oil shock exacerbated post-pandemic inflation.
historical_instances.rates_behavior: The Fed embarked on its most aggressive rate-hiking cycle in 40 years to crush inflation.
historical_instances.resolution: Oil prices gradually ground lower throughout late 2022 and 2023 as supply chains adjusted and strategic reserves were released.

warning_signals: Geopolitical supply disruptions in major producing regions (Middle East, Russia).
Simultaneous spikes in broad commodity indices and headline CPI.
Central banks forced to hike rates into a slowing economy to fight inflation.

macro_theme: Atypical Regime: Reflation, Commodity Supercycle, or Geopolitical Fear

historical_instances.context: Mid-2000s commodity supercycle driven by massive Chinese infrastructure demand and global reflation.
historical_instances.equity_behavior: Global equities were in a strong bull market, particularly emerging markets and commodity producers.
historical_instances.rates_behavior: The Fed was steadily hiking rates (nominal and real yields rising) to cool the booming economy.
historical_instances.resolution: Gold rose alongside real yields because the sheer momentum of the commodity supercycle and inflation expectations overpowered the opportunity cost of higher rates.

historical_instances.context: Structural shift in gold demand driven by massive central bank purchases (especially EM central banks diversifying away from the US dollar) and geopolitical fragmentation.
historical_instances.equity_behavior: Equities performed well, driven by AI and US economic exceptionalism.
historical_instances.rates_behavior: Real yields remained elevated (around 2%) as the Fed held rates high, yet gold repeatedly broke all-time highs.
historical_instances.resolution: Ongoing. The traditional inverse relationship broke down as sovereign buying and fiat debasement fears overwhelmed the mathematical drag of high real yields.

warning_signals: Massive, price-insensitive gold buying by global central banks.
Loss of confidence in fiat currencies or sovereign debt sustainability.
Broad-based commodity supercycles where all hard assets reprice higher simultaneously.

historical_instances.context: The great gold bear market. Following the Volcker shock, inflation was conquered and real interest rates remained structurally high for two decades.
historical_instances.equity_behavior: Massive secular bull market in equities (1982-2000).
historical_instances.rates_behavior: Real yields were highly positive, making zero-yielding gold an unattractive asset.
historical_instances.resolution: Gold bottomed around $250 an ounce in 1999 as central banks (like the Bank of England) sold off reserves at the absolute lows.

historical_instances.context: Post-GFC era of Zero Interest Rate Policy (ZIRP) and Quantitative Easing (QE1, QE2).
historical_instances.equity_behavior: Equities recovered from the 2008 crash, supported by massive central bank liquidity.
historical_instances.rates_behavior: Real yields plunged into negative territory as the Fed pinned nominal rates at zero while inflation expectations recovered.
historical_instances.resolution: Gold surged to a record $1,920 in 2011. The bull market ended when the Fed signaled an eventual end to QE and real yields began to bottom.

historical_instances.context: The Fed pivoted from hiking to cutting in 2019, followed by the massive pandemic QE response in 2020.
historical_instances.equity_behavior: Equities crashed in March 2020 but staged a historic V-shaped recovery driven by stimulus.
historical_instances.rates_behavior: Real yields collapsed to deeply negative levels (-1% on the 10-year TIPS).
historical_instances.resolution: Gold surged past $2,000 in August 2020. The regime ended in 2022 when the Fed began aggressively hiking rates to fight inflation.

warning_signals: Central banks launching new Quantitative Easing programs.
Nominal interest rates pinned at the zero lower bound while inflation rises.
Dovish pivots by the Federal Reserve in response to economic weakness.

historical_instances.context: A multi-year bear market for the US Dollar coinciding with the BRICS boom and a global commodity supercycle.
historical_instances.equity_behavior: Emerging market equities massively outperformed US equities. The MSCI EM index delivered annualized returns near 30%.
historical_instances.rates_behavior: US rates were relatively low following the dot-com bust, encouraging capital to seek higher yields abroad.
historical_instances.resolution: The regime ended abruptly with the 2008 Global Financial Crisis, which triggered a massive flight to safety back into the US Dollar.

historical_instances.context: Synchronized global growth. The US dollar weakened despite the Fed slowly hiking rates, as growth in Europe and EM accelerated.
historical_instances.equity_behavior: EM equities had a banner year, rising over 30% as earnings growth surged and financial conditions loosened.
historical_instances.rates_behavior: Global yields were rising gently, but the growth differential favored non-US assets.
historical_instances.resolution: Ended in 2018 when the US economy accelerated (due to tax cuts) while the rest of the world slowed, causing the dollar to rip higher.

warning_signals: US twin deficits (fiscal and current account) expanding rapidly.
Global growth accelerating synchronously, reducing the need for safe-haven USD.
Commodity prices rising, which improves the terms of trade for EM exporters.

historical_instances.context: Asian Financial Crisis. A strong US dollar exposed massive unhedged dollar-denominated debt in Asian corporate sectors.
historical_instances.equity_behavior: Asian equity markets collapsed (down 30-50%), and currencies broke their pegs to the dollar.
historical_instances.rates_behavior: The Fed had been relatively tight. EM central banks were forced to hike rates to defend currencies, crushing domestic growth.
historical_instances.resolution: IMF bailouts, massive currency devaluations, and eventually the Fed cutting rates in late 1998 following the LTCM collapse.

historical_instances.context: The 'Taper Tantrum'. Fed Chair Bernanke hinted at slowing QE purchases, causing a sudden spike in US yields and the dollar.
historical_instances.equity_behavior: The 'Fragile Five' (Brazil, India, Indonesia, Turkey, South Africa) saw massive capital outflows and equity market selloffs.
historical_instances.rates_behavior: US 10-year yields spiked 100 basis points in a matter of months.
historical_instances.resolution: The Fed walked back its hawkishness, delaying the actual taper, which allowed EM assets to stabilize.

historical_instances.context: Fed quantitative tightening and rate hikes drove the dollar higher, exposing vulnerabilities in Turkey and Argentina.
historical_instances.equity_behavior: EM equities entered a bear market. The Turkish Lira and Argentine Peso collapsed.
historical_instances.rates_behavior: US rates marched steadily higher until Q4 2018.
historical_instances.resolution: The Fed famously 'pivoted' in early 2019, halting rate hikes and ending the dollar squeeze on EM.

warning_signals: Rapid appreciation of the US Dollar Index (DXY).
High levels of US dollar-denominated corporate debt in emerging markets.
Sudden hawkish shifts in Federal Reserve policy causing US yields to spike.

historical_instances.context: The bursting of the Dot-Com bubble. The 10y-2y curve inverted in February 2000.
historical_instances.equity_behavior: The S&P 500 peaked in March 2000, just weeks after the inversion, and subsequently halved in value over the next two years.
historical_instances.rates_behavior: The Fed had hiked rates to 6.5%. As the recession hit, they aggressively slashed rates, causing the curve to rapidly steepen (bull steepening).
historical_instances.resolution: The recession officially began in March 2001. The market bottomed in late 2002.

historical_instances.context: The housing bubble peak. The curve first inverted in December 2005 and stayed inverted for over a year.
historical_instances.equity_behavior: The stock market ignored the inversion for over a year, peaking in October 2007, before collapsing in the Great Financial Crisis.
historical_instances.rates_behavior: The curve actually un-inverted (steepened) just before the recession began, as the market priced in emergency Fed rate cuts.
historical_instances.resolution: The deepest recession since the Great Depression began in December 2007.

historical_instances.context: Late-cycle slowdown exacerbated by the US-China trade war. The curve briefly inverted in August 2019.
historical_instances.equity_behavior: The market experienced a sharp correction in late 2018/2019 but recovered to new highs before the pandemic hit.
historical_instances.rates_behavior: The Fed cut rates three times in 2019 as an 'insurance policy', un-inverting the curve.
historical_instances.resolution: The COVID-19 pandemic caused a massive, albeit brief, recession in early 2020.

warning_signals: The yield curve re-steepening rapidly after a long inversion (bull steepener), signaling the Fed is about to panic-cut rates.
Rising unemployment claims and deteriorating consumer sentiment.
Credit spreads (like high yield bonds) blowing out as corporate default risk rises.
